title: Dice Question (12 vs. 7-7)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-05-stochastic-processes.md

setup.narrative: Two players bet on which event happens FIRST when two dice are rolled repeatedly: Player A wins if a SUM OF 12 appears; Player B wins if TWO CONSECUTIVE 7s appear.
rules: Each roll is independent. P(sum = 12) = 1/36 (only 6+6). P(sum = 7) = 6/36 = 1/6 (six combinations). P(other) = 29/36.
Game ends as soon as either event triggers.
setup.question: What is P(A wins)?

learningObjectives: Set up the Markov chain with two transient states: S (start, previous roll was not 7) and 7 (previous roll was a 7).
Write absorption-probability equations for P(reach 12 before 7-7).
Solve the linear system to get 7/13.

stages.label: Identify the state space
stages.prompt: What's the minimal state we need to track between rolls?

options.text: Whether the PREVIOUS roll was a 7 (since 7-7 requires consecutive 7s).  States: S (no 7 just happened), '7' (just rolled a 7), '12' (absorbing -- A wins), '7-7' (absorbing -- B wins).

stages.explanation: Two transient states (S, '7') and two absorbing states (12, 7-7). 'Whether the previous was a 7' is enough because 7-7 just needs the next roll to also be 7.

stages.label: Write the absorption equations
stages.prompt: Let a_S = P(A wins from state S) and a_7 = P(A wins from state 7). Set up the equations.
stages.explanation: From S: roll 12 (absorb to A's win), roll 7 (transition to '7' state), roll other (stay at S). From '7': roll 12 (A wins), roll another 7 (B wins via 7-7 absorption), roll other (back to S).

stages.label: Solve the system
stages.prompt: Substitute a_7 from the second equation into the first and solve for a_S.

stages.label: Commit
stages.explanation: P(A wins) = 7/13 ≈ 0.538. A is slightly favored despite the lower per-roll probability of 12 vs. 7 -- because B needs TWO consecutive 7s, which is much rarer than A's single 12.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Generalize: A bets on event X (prob p per roll), B bets on TWO consecutive Y (prob q per roll). Find a closed form for P(A wins).
What's E[number of rolls until the game ends]?
Verify by Method 1 (conditional probability without explicit Markov chain) -- gives the same answer.
